Name: pac-bayes_latency_optimization
Title: Latency-Aware PAC-Bayes Inference: A Framework for Optimizing Latency Under Computational Constraints
Short Hypothesis: Implementing a PAC-Bayes framework specifically tailored for latency-aware ML models can significantly improve adaptive inference performance while adhering to strict computational budgets.
Related Work: Existing research has explored PAC-Bayes frameworks primarily in the context of control policies and generalization in novel environments. Notable works include Majumdar et al. (2018), which focus on generalization guarantees for robotic control, and Thakur et al. (2019), which discuss generalization properties in supervised learning. However, these studies do not address the specific integration of PAC-Bayes principles with latency-aware model optimization, particularly under stringent computational constraints, which is a critical gap this proposal seeks to fill.
Abstract: This proposal introduces a novel framework for applying PAC-Bayes theory to latency-aware machine learning inference. By framing inference optimization as a bounded computational budget problem, we aim to derive enforceable protocols that ensure strict adherence to latency constraints while leveraging probabilistic generalization guarantees. This work will explore a Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism to minimize hidden-state perturbation during inference, optimizing throughput while ensuring accuracy. We will conduct experiments to evaluate the performance of our proposed framework against traditional methods, focusing on the trade-offs between throughput and accuracy under varying latency constraints. This approach not only addresses a fundamental gap in the literature but also aims to advance the state-of-the-art in efficient machine learning inference, particularly for resource-constrained environments.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Develop a baseline latency-aware model using traditional methods and compare its performance against our proposed PAC-Bayes framework under identical computational budgets.

Experiment 2: Implement the Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism and evaluate its effectiveness in reducing latency while maintaining accuracy across different tasks (e.g., image classification, text processing).
Experiment 3: Conduct ablation studies to quantify the impact of various hyperparameters on the latency-accuracy trade-off, ensuring a comprehensive analysis of the proposed framework's performance.
Evaluation metrics will include inference latency, accuracy, and throughput, with a focus on finding the optimal balance along the Pareto frontier.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential overfitting to specific tasks if the framework is not sufficiently generalized across diverse datasets.
Challenges in capturing the trade-offs between latency and accuracy due to inherent model complexities.
The requirement for strict computational resources may limit the scalability of the proposed methods in real-world applications.
